title: नवग्रह शान्ति स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री नवग्रह शान्ति स्तोत्रम् ॥

जपाकुसुमसंकाशं काश्यपेयं महाद्युतिम्।
तमोरिं सर्वपापघ्नं प्रणतोऽस्मि दिवाकरम्॥१॥

दधिशंखतुषाराभं क्षीरोदार्णवसम्भवम्।
नमामि शशिनं सोमं शम्भोर्मुकुटभूषणम्॥२॥

धरणीगर्भसम्भूतं विद्युत्कान्तिसमप्रभम्।
कुमारं शक्तिहस्तं तं मंगलं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥३॥

प्रियंगुकलिकाश्यामं रूपेणाप्रतिमं बुधम्।
सौम्यं सौम्यगुणोपेतं तं बुधं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥४॥

देवानां च ऋषीणां च गुरुं काञ्चनसन्निभम्।
बुद्धिभूतं त्रिलोकेशं तं नमामि बृहस्पतिम्॥५॥

हिमकुन्दमृणालाभं दैत्यानां परमं गुरुम्।
सर्वशास्त्रप्रवक्तारं भार्गवं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥६॥

नीलाञ्जनसमाभासं रविपुत्रं यमाग्रजम्।
छायामार्तण्डसम्भूतं तं नमामि शनैश्चरम्॥७॥

अर्धकायं महावीर्यं चन्द्रादित्यविमर्दनम्।
सिंहिकागर्भसम्भूतं तं राहुं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥८॥

पलाशपुष्पसंकाशं तारकाग्रहमस्तकम्।
रौद्रं रौद्रात्मकं घोरं तं केतुं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥९॥

इति व्यासमुखोद्गीतं यः पठेत् सुसमाहितः।
दिवा वा यदि वा रात्रौ विघ्नशान्तिर्भविष्यति॥१०॥

॥ इति श्री व्यासविरचितं नवग्रह शान्ति स्तोत्रं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Navagraha Shanti Stotram ||

Japa-kusuma-sankasham Kashyapeyam Maha-dyutim.
Tamo-rim Sarva-papa-ghnam Pranato-asmi Divakaram.||1||

Dadhi-shankha-tusharabham Kshirodarnava-sambhavam.
Namami Shashinam Somam Shambhor-mukuta-bhushanam.||2||

Dharani-garbha-sambhutam Vidyut-kanti-sama-prabham.
Kumaram Shakti-hastam Tam Mangalam Pranamami-aham.||3||

Priyangu-kalika-shyamam Rupena-apratimam Budham.
Saumyam Saumya-guna-upetam Tam Budham Pranamami-aham.||4||

Devanam Cha Rishinam Cha Gurum Kanchana-sannibham.
Buddhi-bhutam Trilokesham Tam Namami Brihaspatim.||5||

Hima-kunda-mrinalabham Daityanam Paramam Gurum.
Sarva-shastra-pravaktaram Bhargavam Pranamami-aham.||6||

Nilanjana-sama-abhasam Ravi-putram Yama-agrajam.
Chhaya-martanda-sambhutam Tam Namami Shanaishcharam.||7||

Ardha-kayam Maha-viryam Chandra-aditya-vimardanam.
Simhika-garbha-sambhutam Tam Rahum Pranamami-aham.||8||

Palasha-pushpa-sankasham Taraka-graha-mastakam.
Raudram Raudra-atmakam Ghoram Tam Ketum Pranamami-aham.||9||

Iti Vyasa-mukha-udgitam Yah Pathet Su-samahitah.
Diva Va Yadi Va Ratrau Vighna-shantir-bhavishyati.||10||

|| Iti Shri Vyasa-virachitam Navagraha Shanti Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning: जपाकुसुम जैसे वर्ण वाले, काश्यप के पुत्र, महातेजस्वी, अन्धकार के शत्रु, सर्वपापनाशक दिवाकर (सूर्य) को प्रणाम। दही-शंख-तुषार जैसे श्वेत, क्षीरसागर से उत्पन्न, शम्भु मुकुट भूषण सोम (चन्द्र) को नमन। पृथिवी गर्भ से उत्पन्न, विद्युत जैसी कान्ति वाले, शक्तिधारी कुमार मंगल को प्रणाम। प्रियंगु कलिका जैसे श्याम, अप्रतिम रूप वाले, सौम्य बुध को प्रणाम। देवगुरु बृहस्पति, शुक्राचार्य, शनैश्चर, राहु, केतु को प्रणाम। व्यास मुख से निकला यह स्तोत्र पढ़ने से विघ्नशान्ति होती है।
benefits: Composed by Sage Vyasa, the most authoritative Navagraha prayer
Individually describes and pacifies each of the nine planets
Classical tradition invokes this for removal of types of planetary obstacles and afflictions
Can be recited day or night for immediate graha shanti
Essential for those undergoing difficult dasha periods
Most widely recited navagraha stotra in Hindu tradition
description: Navagraha Shanti Stotram — नवग्रह शान्ति स्तोत्रम् — is a devotional hymn of praise offered to the Nine Planets (Navagraha). Drawn from Vyasa, this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to can be recited day or night for immediate graha shanti as well as individually describes and pacify each of the nine planets. Additionally, it is known to composed by Sage Vyasa, the most authoritative Navagraha prayer. This prayer is traditionally recited on Sunday (Ravivar), Monday (Somvar), Tuesday (Mangalvar), Wednesday (Budhvar), Thursday (Guruvar), Friday (Shukravar), and Saturday (Shanivar) to invoke divine grace. With 10 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Navagraha Shanti Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Navagraha Shanti Stotram by Vyasa in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning. Powerful prayer to pacify all nine planets for peace and obstacle removal.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Navagraha Shanti Stotram and who composed it?
seoFaqs.answer: The Navagraha Shanti Stotram is a ten-verse Sanskrit hymn composed by Sage Vyasa (Veda Vyasa, the compiler of the Vedas and author of the Mahabharata and the Puranas) for pacifying the nine planetary deities of the Hindu cosmos. It contains one anushtup verse for each of the nine planets — Surya (Sun), Chandra (Moon), Mangal (Mars), Budha (Mercury), Brihaspati (Jupiter), Shukra (Venus), Shani (Saturn), Rahu (the ascending lunar node), and Ketu (the descending lunar node) — followed by a single phalashruti verse promising that the recitation will pacify all obstacles. It is the most widely recited Navagraha prayer across all Hindu denominations and is the standard text used in Navagraha-shanti pujas. (Sources: Skanda Purana; Brahma Purana Navagraha section; vedadhara.com Vyasa Navagraha.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is this stotra called Vyasa-virachita and what is its authority?
seoFaqs.answer: The closing line — Iti Vyasa-mukha-udgitam — declares that this stotra was sung from the mouth of Vyasa himself. Sage Vyasa's authorship gives the text the highest scriptural authority in the Smarta tradition, equivalent to the Puranic compositions he authored. Because Vyasa is regarded as a partial avatar of Vishnu and as the rishi-redactor of the Vedas, any stotra ascribed to him is treated as apta-vakya — the word of an authoritative seer — and is recited without question by all four traditional sampradayas (Smarta, Shaiva, Vaishnava and Shakta). For this reason, even temples that do not normally practice graha-puja install Navagraha shrines and use this Vyasa text for their daily archana. (Sources: classical Smarta tradition; Vyasa-Brahmasutra connection.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between Navagraha Shanti Stotram and Navagraha Stotra?
seoFaqs.answer: The two titles refer to essentially the same nine-verse-per-planet Vyasa composition, with minor differences in arrangement and phalashruti length. This Navagraha Shanti Stotram presents the nine planet verses in continuous flow plus one closing phalashruti verse (10 verses total). The companion Navagraha Stotra on this site presents the same nine verses with each verse explicitly headed by the planet's name (Surya, Chandra, Mangal etc.) and includes an extended three-verse phalashruti about pacification of bad dreams, ailments and theft-fire dangers. Both are equally valid and are interchangeably recited; the distinction is editorial rather than textual. Devotees may use whichever presentation they prefer. (Sources: comparative Vyasa Navagraha Stotra editions; stotra-nidhi.com.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should one recite Navagraha Shanti Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The phalashruti specifies that the stotra may be recited divaa va yadi va ratrau — by day or by night — and produces immediate vighna-shanti (pacification of obstacles). Practical recommendations: recite all ten verses daily as part of morning sandhya for general planetary harmony; intensify recitation on the weekday ruled by the afflicting planet (Sunday for Sun, Monday for Moon, Tuesday for Mars, Wednesday for Mercury, Thursday for Jupiter, Friday for Venus, Saturday for Saturn, and Saturdays or Wednesdays for Rahu and Ketu). For severe afflictions during a difficult mahadasha or sade-sati, recite 108 times daily for one mandala (40 days). The stotra is mandatorily included in any formal Navagraha-homa or Graha-shanti puja and is recited at the start and end of the puja. (Sources: phalashruti verse; Navagraha-shanti homa paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: Which planet is described in each verse of the stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: The nine planet verses follow the canonical order: Verse 1 — Surya (Sun), red as the hibiscus flower, son of Kashyapa; Verse 2 — Chandra (Moon), white as curd, conch and snow, born of the milk ocean, ornament of Shiva's crown; Verse 3 — Mangal (Mars), born of the earth's womb, lightning-bright, Kumara holding the Shakti weapon; Verse 4 — Budha (Mercury), dark as a Priyangu bud, of unparalleled gentle form; Verse 5 — Brihaspati (Jupiter), guru of devas and rishis, golden, lord of the three worlds; Verse 6 — Shukra (Venus), white as snow-jasmine, guru of the Daityas, expounder of all shastras; Verse 7 — Shani (Saturn), dark as black antimony, son of Surya, elder brother of Yama; Verse 8 — Rahu, half-bodied, eclipser of Sun and Moon, born of Simhika's womb; Verse 9 — Ketu, dark as palasha flower, head of the meteor-grahas, terrifying. (Sources: Vyasa Navagraha Stotra; Brihat Parashara Hora Shastra deva-vibhaga.)

seoFaqs.question: Can Navagraha Shanti Stotram replace individual planetary mantras?
seoFaqs.answer: It can serve as a comprehensive daily practice but is best used alongside individual planetary mantras during specific dasha periods rather than as a substitute. The strength of this stotra is its all-in-one coverage — a single five-minute recitation honours all nine grahas equitably, which is especially useful when a person does not know exactly which planet is troubling them, or when multiple planets are simultaneously afflicting the chart. For a confirmed dasha-affliction of one planet, jyotisha texts recommend supplementing the stotra with that planet's beej mantra (e.g. Om Sham Shanaishcharaya Namah for Saturn) recited 108 times daily, the planet's specific stotra (Aditya Hridayam for Sun, Chandra Stotra for Moon etc.), and the appropriate yajna or dana. (Sources: Brihat Parashara graha-shanti chapter; classical jyotisha-parihara manuals.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the significance of reciting this stotra during Sade Sati or Mahadasha?
seoFaqs.answer: Sade Sati is the seven-and-a-half year transit period of Saturn through the moon-sign and the signs adjacent to it, considered one of the most challenging astrological periods. Mahadasha is the multi-year ruling period of any planet in the Vimshottari dasha system (Saturn 19 years, Venus 20, Sun 6, etc.). During these intense planetary periods, the Vyasa Navagraha Shanti Stotram is recommended as a daily protective recitation because it pacifies not just the dasha-lord but all nine grahas simultaneously, ensuring that no other planet exacerbates the difficulty. Devotees often combine the daily stotra with weekly visits to the Navagraha shrine of any major Shiva or Vishnu temple, donations of the affected planet's items (sesame for Saturn, white cloth for Venus etc.), and observance of the planet's weekday vrat. (Sources: classical Vimshottari dasha texts; Sade Sati parihara traditions.)
